3: Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used in recent decades to compare the carbon footprint (CF) of milk from different produc- tion systems (O'Brien et al., 2014). Reduction of milk CF has been achieved more by increasing milk production per cow than by

Abbreviations: AS, Alfalfa silage; CF, Carbon footprint; CH4, methane; CO2-e, Carbon dioxide equivalent; CS, Corn silage; FNDF, Forage neutral detergent fiber; FPCM, Fat-and-protein corrected milk; GHG, greenhouse gas; HF, High forage; LCA, Life cycle assessment; LF, Low forage; NDF, Neutral detergent fiber; NH3, Ammonia; N20, Nitrous oxide.

reducing daily emission of greenhouse gases (GHG; Knapp et al., 2014). For example, the CF of milk production in the intensive systems common in the developed world is much lower than the extensive system used primarily in the developing word [1.29 VS. 6.60 kg carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-e)/kg of fat-and-protein corrected milk (FPCM)] primarily due to large differences in milk production and milk to meat ratios (FAO and GDP, 2018). Even within intensive systems of the developed world, the CF of milk production may vary widely depending on choice of functional unit, system boundary, LCA methodology (attributional VS. conse- quential), methods of calculation (Zanten et al., 2018); methods of co-products allocation (e.g., economic VS. mass) and other as- sumptions such as global warming potential of GHGs (Baldini et al., 2018). For instance, CF of milk across 10 studies ranged from 0.84 to 1.5 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM for intensive confined and pasture-based systems using a cradle-to-farmgate LCA approach (Wattiaux et al.,
1 Current affiliation: Department of Animal Science, University of California- Davis, Davis, CA 95616, USA.

2019). Furthermore, a cradle-to-grave LCA conducted in the United States reported that the CF of milk production and consumption was 2.04 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM (Thoma et al., 2013). The latter study also identified enteric methane (CH4), manure management, and feed production as three hotspots contributing to approximately 70% of the United States milk CF (Thoma et al., 2013). Focusing on reducing the emission from each of these three emission sources is important, but one cannot assume additivity of mitigation strate- gies as emission tradeoffs among system components have been shown to be substantial under certain conditions (Gerber et al., 2013). Unfortunately, there are limited studies evaluating the impact of mitigation strategies on these three GHG emissions hotspots considered together.

Among enteric CH4 mitigation strategies, feeding and nutri- tional strategies have been extensively studied because of their immediate response (short-term impact) and ease of applicability (Knapp et al., 2014). Some of the recently studied and potential nutritional strategies for mitigating enteric CH4 emission include replacement of alfalfa silage (AS) with corn silage (CS; Hassanat et al., 2013), replacement of AS with timothy silage (Hassanat et al., 2014), replacement of CS with barley silage (Benchaar et al., 2014), manipulating forage to concentrate ratio (Aguerre et al., 2011), and replacement of starch with dextrose (Sun et al., 2019). Several studies also pointed out that there might be a carry-over effect of diet on manure GHG emissions (Külling et al., 2001) due to changing manure composition such as organic matter content (Uddin et al., 2020b), form and content of nitrogen, and carbon to nitrogen ratio (Wattiaux et al., 2019).

In the great lakes region of the United States, dairy producers are shifting toward producing and feeding a greater amount of CS at the expense of AS mainly to support increasing herd size without concomitant change in land-base (Martin et al., 2017). Effects of the changes in dietary forages and associated change in land use (cropping system) on CF of milk have been modeled at the system level (Little et al., 2017). Separately, dairy breeds (Jersey and Hol- stein) appear to have similar enteric CH4 intensity (g/kg FPCM; Uddin et al., 2020a). In contrast, simulation studies suggested that smaller body size and greater fertility (Capper and Cady, 2012) of Jerseys compared to Holsteins may contribute to a lower system- wide estimate of CF when expressed per unit of cheese produc- tion in the former compared to the latter.

Furthermore, estimates of GHG emission from manure man- agement remains highly variable and uncertain. For example, Baldini et al. (2018) reported a 21% under-estimation of GHG emissions when using Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) based emission factors compared to empirical data. Hence, the site-specific inventory data including direct measurement of emission factors rather than estimation using IPPC equations might capture the differences in regional characteristics or farm man- agement practices resulting in accurate LCA results (Owen and Silver, 2015).

Aiming to evaluate the effects of different dietary scenarios on cradle-to-farmgate GHG emissions, we previously conducted one experiment subdivided into three interconnected studies. The first study evaluated the effects of four dietary treatment combinations [two forage neutral detergent fiber (FNDF) levels X two forage sources] and two breeds (Holstein and Jersey) on enteric CH4 emission, lactation performances, and manure yield (Uddin et al., 2020a). The second study measured GHG emissions during stor- age of manure collected in the first study, whereas the third study measured GHG emissions following manure land application at the end of the second study (Uddin et al., 2020b). The objective of this study was to compare the CF of milk using a cradle-to-farmgate LCA approach for four dietary scenarios of lactating Holstein and Jersey cows using our previously measured enteric CH4 and GHG
emissions during manure storage, and after field-application under the management practices and climate conditions prevailing in the Great Lakes region of North America.

Even within intensive systems of the developed world, the CF of milk production may vary widely depending on choice of functional unit, system boundary, LCA methodology (attributional VS. conse- quential), methods of calculation (Zanten et al., 2018); methods of co-products allocation (e.g., economic VS. mass) and other as- sumptions such as global warming potential of GHGs (Baldini et al., 2018). For instance, CF of milk across 10 studies ranged from 0.84 to 1.5 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM for intensive confined and pasture-based systems using a cradle-to-farmgate LCA approach (Wattiaux et al.,
1 Current affiliation: Department of Animal Science, University of California- Davis, Davis, CA 95616, USA.

2019). Furthermore, a cradle-to-grave LCA conducted in the United States reported that the CF of milk production and consumption was 2.04 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM (Thoma et al., 2013). The latter study also identified enteric methane (CH4), manure management, and feed production as three hotspots contributing to approximately 70% of the United States milk CF (Thoma et al., 2013). Focusing on reducing the emission from each of these three emission sources is important, but one cannot assume additivity of mitigation strate- gies as emission tradeoffs among system components have been shown to be substantial under certain conditions (Gerber et al., 2013). Unfortunately, there are limited studies evaluating the impact of mitigation strategies on these three GHG emissions hotspots considered together.

Among enteric CH4 mitigation strategies, feeding and nutri- tional strategies have been extensively studied because of their immediate response (short-term impact) and ease of applicability (Knapp et al., 2014). Some of the recently studied and potential nutritional strategies for mitigating enteric CH4 emission include replacement of alfalfa silage (AS) with corn silage (CS; Hassanat et al., 2013), replacement of AS with timothy silage (Hassanat et al., 2014), replacement of CS with barley silage (Benchaar et al., 2014), manipulating forage to concentrate ratio (Aguerre et al., 2011), and replacement of starch with dextrose (Sun et al., 2019). Several studies also pointed out that there might be a carry-over effect of diet on manure GHG emissions (Külling et al., 2001) due to changing manure composition such as organic matter content (Uddin et al., 2020b), form and content of nitrogen, and carbon to nitrogen ratio (Wattiaux et al., 2019).

In the great lakes region of the United States, dairy producers are shifting toward producing and feeding a greater amount of CS at the expense of AS mainly to support increasing herd size without concomitant change in land-base (Martin et al., 2017). Effects of the changes in dietary forages and associated change in land use (cropping system) on CF of milk have been modeled at the system level (Little et al., 2017). Separately, dairy breeds (Jersey and Hol- stein) appear to have similar enteric CH4 intensity (g/kg FPCM; Uddin et al., 2020a). In contrast, simulation studies suggested that smaller body size and greater fertility (Capper and Cady, 2012) of Jerseys compared to Holsteins may contribute to a lower system- wide estimate of CF when expressed per unit of cheese produc- tion in the former compared to the latter.

Furthermore, estimates of GHG emission from manure man- agement remains highly variable and uncertain. For example, Baldini et al. (2018) reported a 21% under-estimation of GHG emissions when using Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) based emission factors compared to empirical data. Hence, the site-specific inventory data including direct measurement of emission factors rather than estimation using IPPC equations might capture the differences in regional characteristics or farm man- agement practices resulting in accurate LCA results (Owen and Silver, 2015).

Aiming to evaluate the effects of different dietary scenarios on cradle-to-farmgate GHG emissions, we previously conducted one experiment subdivided into three interconnected studies. The first study evaluated the effects of four dietary treatment combinations [two forage neutral detergent fiber (FNDF) levels X two forage sources] and two breeds (Holstein and Jersey) on enteric CH4 emission, lactation performances, and manure yield (Uddin et al., 2020a). The second study measured GHG emissions during stor- age of manure collected in the first study, whereas the third study measured GHG emissions following manure land application at the end of the second study (Uddin et al., 2020b). The objective of this study was to compare the CF of milk using a cradle-to-farmgate LCA approach for four dietary scenarios of lactating Holstein and Jersey cows using our previously measured enteric CH4 and GHG
emissions during manure storage, and after field-application under the management practices and climate conditions prevailing in the Great Lakes region of North America.

10: An attributional LCA was performed using SimaPro 9.0 as per ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; 2006b) following Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) Livestock Environmental Assessment Protocol guidelines (FAO, 2016a,b). The mid-point impact assessment was calculated based on IPCC 2013 single issue method for GHG emissions (Myhre et al., 2013). The CF result was expressed as kg CO2-e per kg FPCM. The global warming potential used for converting CH4 and N20 to CO2 were 28 and 265, respectively over 100-year horizon (Myhre et al., 2013).

The system boundaries, functional unit, co-product allocation method, as well as the origin of the inventory data related to animal performances, enteric CH4, GHG from feed production in the field, GHG from manure management (storage and after field applica- tion), and GHG from farm energy consumption are described in each of the following sections.
11: <header>2.1. System boundaries and description of the modeled dairy herd </header>

In this study, the system boundary included emissions from enteric fermentation, manure management (from barn to field including indirect N20 emissions from NH3 volatilization), on-farm feed production, purchased feed production, and production and transportation of energy and materials to the farm (Fig. 1). Capital goods such as buildings, machinery production related emissions were not accounted for in this study. Similarly, CO2 from animals' breath was not included since it is a release of CO2 previously captured through plant photosynthesis. However, our study accounted for CO2 emissions from burning fossil fuels for farm electricity to run milking machine, feeder, cooling fan, and manure handling.
We modeled an average size Wisconsin dairy herd consisting of 118 lactating cows, 22 dry cows (USDA-NASS, 2017), 56 growing

heifers over one year of age and 63 growing heifers under one year of age (Rotz et al., 2018). For all scenarios, a similar herd structure was assumed for both Holstein and Jersey breeds. Body weight, dry matter intake, FPCM and manure yield of lactating cows of each breed were those measured in companion study in which primip- arous Holstein and Jersey cows were fed four diets (Table 1). The dry matter intake and body weight of non-lactating cattle were obtained from the literature (Supplementary Table S1; NRC, 2001).

The four dietary scenarios used for lactating cows of the modeled herd were the experimental diets used in Uddin et al. (2020a), namely, diets with two levels of FNDF from either AS or CS as the main FNDF source. Dietary FNDF levels were 19 and 24% (dry matter basis) respectively referred to as low forage (LF) and high forage (HF) (Supplementary Table S2). The sources of FNDF were either from AS or CS (i.e., 70:30 or 30:70 ratio of AS NDF:CS NDF). Although FNDF varied, total NDF (averaging 31% across diets) was kept similar between LF and HF, and AS and CS diets by adding non-forage NDF from soyhulls. Forage to concentrate ratios were 55:45 and 68:32 for LF and HF diets, respectively while maintaining similar crude protein (17%) and starch (23%) concentrations across diets. Further details of the diets including chemical composition have been described in Uddin et al. (2020a). The NRC (2001) guidelines were used to formulate the diets of non-lactating ani- mals using dry matter intake and body weight information from Akins (2016).

15: The functional unit was 1 kg FPCM [FPCM (kg/d) = milk pro- duction (kg/d) X (0.1226 X milk fat % + 0.0776 X true protein % + 0.2534) (IDF, 2015);] using milk production and composition measured for each dietary scenario by Uddin et al. (2020a).
16: An attributional allocation method was adopted based on bio- physical relationship between milk and meat. Using data from each scenario, allocation factor for milk was calculated as: 1-6.04 X (kilograms of live weight sold/kilograms of milk pro- duced), and allocation factor for meat was calculated as: 1- allo- cation factor for milk (IDF, 2015). A complimentary analysis was performed to determine the effect of using alternative allocation methods such as no allocation (i.e., 100% emissions were allocated to milk) or economic allocation [based on income generated by milk and meat as percentage of total income using USDA-NASS, 2017 data]. 19: Emission factors for enteric CH4 are shown in Table 2. Enteric CH4 of lactating cows was measured in Uddin et al. (2020a) whereas enteric CH4 for non-lactating animals was predicted with the equation of Niu et al. (2018) using dry matter intake and dietary NDF information.
20: All forage production (CS and AS) and corn grain were assumed to be produced on-farm, a common practice in Wisconsin, and all other concentrate feed ingredients were assumed to be purchased from local sources. The total land required for on-farm AS, CS and corn grain production under different dietary and breed scenarios were calculated based on dry matter yield of AS (6263 kg dry matter/ha), CS (16,858 kg dry matter/ha) and corn grain (9768 kg dry matter/ha) as per USDA-NASS (2017) shown in Supplementary Table S3. Chemical composition of AS and CS, animal intake and dietary composition were taken from Uddin et al. (2020a). All manure was assumed to be recycled as fertilizer for forage and grain production on the farm assuming crop availability of 50, 80, and 80% for N, P, and K, respectively (Laboski and Peter, 2012). The amounts of N, P and K supplied by manure were calculated using the data of Uddin et al. (2020b) for manure yield information, and manure N, P, and K concentrations presented in Laboski and Peter (2012). Given our farm scenario the supply of manure nutrients was insufficient to meet crop requirements, and the deficits (range across scenarios: 25-56% for N, 57-63% for P and 65-74% for K) were assumed to be purchased as chemical fertilizer.

The emissions for on-farm feed production were calculated based on off-farm inputs (e.g., chemical fertilizer, herbicide, pesti- cide etc.) needed for AS, CS and corn grain production as shown in Table 3 (Ecoinvent version 3.0). We assumed regular tillage on the farm, and fuels needed for tillage were incorporated into the inputs needed for feed production (Table 3). The emission factors for soybean meal, molasses, blood meal, and vitamin and minerals premix were adapted from Adom et al. (2012), and Adom et al. (2013) reported for the Midwestern region (Supplementary Table S4). Most of the feed ingredients were assumed to be sourced locally and thus transportation distance was relatively short (24.8 Kannan et al., 2016). The emissions from the return of an empty feed truck were not accounted for in this study. The transportation emissions for a 32-metric ton size freight truck was based on Ecoinvent database (version 3.0).

aFour dietary scenarios: Where, LF = Low forage diets containing 19.0% forage neutral detergent fiber; HF = High forage diets containing 24.0% forage neutral detergent fiber, AS = Alfalfa silage-based diets with a 70:30 ratio of alfalfa silage neutral detergent fiber:corn silage neutral detergent fiber; CS = Corn silage-based diets with a 30:70 ratio of alfalfa silage neutral detergent fiber:corn silage neutral detergent fiber. Dry cows and heifers were assumed to be fed the same diet under different dietary scenarios, emission factors for non-lactating were similar across scenarios. Manure was stored over 50 days before land application and thus, storage emission factors were for 50 days period. dield emissions was conducted over 50 days both in fall and spring followed by land application (surface application) and then, the field emission factors were extrapolated to annual emissions. Calculated indirect N20 due to NH3 volatilization accounting for all possible NH3 sources (during manure storage, land application of N both from manure and chemical fertilizer). However, barn NH3 was not accounted for in this study since we did not have barn NH3 emission measurements due to direct collection of feces and urine separately (details can be found in Uddin and Wattiaux, 2020). Calculated indirect N20 due to NO volatilization accounting for all possible NO sources both from manure and chemical fertilizer. SA farm consists of 118 lactating cows, 22 dry cows, 56 heifers over one year of age and 63 heifers under one year of age.

30: Presence of uncertainty may create difficulties to compare LCA scenarios results. To evaluate uncertainty, a modified null hypoth- esis significance testing has been recommended by Beltran et al. (2018), however, these authors concluded that further research was necessary. In this study, a Monte Carlo simulation with 1000 iterations was performed on SimaPro 9.0 to determine the standard deviation and 95% confidence interval of milk CF by varying pa- rameters such as FPCM, dry matter intake, enteric CH4 emission,

CH4 and N20 emissions during storage and after field applications. Variations were accounted for using standard deviations of pa- rameters which were calculated from standard errors as reported in companion studies (Uddin et al. 2020a, 2020b; Uddin and Wattiaux, 2021). Furthermore, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed to identify the emission sources that have greatest influ- ence on milk CF. As per the method described by Rotz et al. (2020), a sensitivity index (ratio of a percentage change in milk CF due to 10% change of an input emission source) was calculated for each GHG sources of each scenario. This sensitivity index ranges from 0 to 1. An input component with a sensitivity index close to zero means changing this component would not change the output much. Unfortunately, this sensitivity index does not capture possible in- teractions between input components.

32: <header>3.1. Carbon footprint of milk under different dietary scenarios </header>

The milk CF of different dietary scenarios including the contri- bution of each process (e.g., enteric CH4, and manure chain GHG emissions) on overall CF is shown in Table 4. Enteric CH4 intensity between LF and HF-fed cows was very similar. On the contrary, we found 34% greater manure management related GHG emissions for LF than HF-fed COWS particularly when FNDF was mainly coming from CS (i.e., LF-CS dietary combination). This greater emission of GHG (mainly in the form of CH4 during storage of manure) for LF than HF-fed COWS might be associated with the inherent charac- teristics of manure (e.g., acid detergent fiber and NDF concentra- tions; Uddin et al., 2020b) since manure volume was similar between LF and HF-diets (Uddin et al., 2020a). This inherent manure characteristics were again influenced by substantially greater concentrations of soyhulls in LF than HF-diets (15.7 VS 6.6% of dietary dry matter; Uddin et al., 2020a). Additionally, soyhulls fiber contains less lignin than forage fiber. Therefore,

proportionately greater content of highly digestible soyhulls fiber in the manure of LF than HF-fed cows might have enhanced CH4 production during long term anaerobic fermentation of manure (Uddin et al., 2020b). In agreement with this interpretation, Benchaar and Hassanat (2019) also reported a 55% greater CH4 emissions during storage of manure from the cows fed highly digestible brown mid-rib CS compared to conventional CS. Emis- sions due to feed production and burning fossil fuel for electricity were similar between LF and HF-fed cows but indirect N20 emis- sion was greater for LF than HF-fed cows. As a result, overall CF for LF-fed cows was 10% greater than HF-fed cows (1.49 VS. 1.35 kg CO2- e/kg FPCM). Most importantly, the difference in CF between LF and HF diets was minimal (1.4%) within AS-based diets but substantial (19%) within CS-based diets (i.e., probable interaction between FNDF level and FNDF source but difficult to test statistically in this LCA study). Nevertheless, percentage contribution of each process (i.e., GHG source) on overall milk CF clearly showed the trade-off between enteric CH4 and manure management related GHG emissions (i.e., lower enteric CH4 but greater manure chain GHG for LF than HF-diets; Fig. 2) highlighting the importance of farm-level evaluation of mitigation strategies (Kebreab et al., 2019). Compared to AS, CS-fed cows had approximately 13% greater manure management related GHG emission particularly CH4 emission during storage of manure (0.49 VS. 0.42 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM for CS and AS, respectively). This greater manure GHG emissions for CS than AS-fed cows were partially compensated by lower enteric CH4, feed production related GHG and emissions for farm energy. Thus, the forage source had minimal impact on milk CF, which was slightly (~2.5%) greater for CS-fed than AS-fed cows (1.44 VS. 1.42 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM). A similar finding was also reported by Little et al. (2017) who concluded similar CF for AS and CS-fed cows under Canadian production system. However, difference in milk CF

Fig. 2. The percentage contribution of different greenhouse gas sources to cradle-to- farmgate carbon footprint [kg CO2-e/kg fat-and-protein corrected milk (FPCM)] for different dietary and breed scenarios when emissions were allocated between milk and meat using economic method. Carbon footprint for low-forage, high-forage, alfalfa silage and corn silage-based diets were 1.49, 1.35, 1.40 and 1.44 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM, respectively. Carbon footprint for Holsteins and Jerseys were 1.47 and 1.41 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM, respectively.

between CS and AS seemingly depended on FNDF level (i.e., po- tential interaction between FNDF level and FNDF source). Within LF diets, the milk CF for CS was 10% greater than AS. Conversely, milk CF for CS was 6% lower than AS within HF diets. In contrast to forage level, percentage contribution of each process on overall CF was similar between silage sources (Fig. 2). It should be noted that changes in soil carbon due to changes in cropping system was not accounted for in the calculation of CF in this study due to un- availability of long-term data. A recent study by Little et al. (2017) concluded that AS-based system could potentially store more car- bon than CS-based dairy production systems. Future studies comparing two different forage based dairy production systems should account for reliable estimates of soil carbon change.

Additionally, soyhulls fiber contains less lignin than forage fiber. Therefore,

proportionately greater content of highly digestible soyhulls fiber in the manure of LF than HF-fed cows might have enhanced CH4 production during long term anaerobic fermentation of manure (Uddin et al., 2020b). In agreement with this interpretation, Benchaar and Hassanat (2019) also reported a 55% greater CH4 emissions during storage of manure from the cows fed highly digestible brown mid-rib CS compared to conventional CS. Emis- sions due to feed production and burning fossil fuel for electricity were similar between LF and HF-fed cows but indirect N20 emis- sion was greater for LF than HF-fed cows. As a result, overall CF for LF-fed cows was 10% greater than HF-fed cows (1.49 VS. 1.35 kg CO2- e/kg FPCM). Most importantly, the difference in CF between LF and HF diets was minimal (1.4%) within AS-based diets but substantial (19%) within CS-based diets (i.e., probable interaction between FNDF level and FNDF source but difficult to test statistically in this LCA study). Nevertheless, percentage contribution of each process (i.e., GHG source) on overall milk CF clearly showed the trade-off between enteric CH4 and manure management related GHG emissions (i.e., lower enteric CH4 but greater manure chain GHG for LF than HF-diets; Fig. 2) highlighting the importance of farm-level evaluation of mitigation strategies (Kebreab et al., 2019). Compared to AS, CS-fed cows had approximately 13% greater manure management related GHG emission particularly CH4 emission during storage of manure (0.49 VS. 0.42 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM for CS and AS, respectively). This greater manure GHG emissions for CS than AS-fed cows were partially compensated by lower enteric CH4, feed production related GHG and emissions for farm energy. Thus, the forage source had minimal impact on milk CF, which was slightly (~2.5%) greater for CS-fed than AS-fed cows (1.44 VS. 1.42 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM). A similar finding was also reported by Little et al. (2017) who concluded similar CF for AS and CS-fed cows under Canadian production system. However, difference in milk CF

between CS and AS seemingly depended on FNDF level (i.e., po- tential interaction between FNDF level and FNDF source). Within LF diets, the milk CF for CS was 10% greater than AS. Conversely, milk CF for CS was 6% lower than AS within HF diets. In contrast to forage level, percentage contribution of each process on overall CF was similar between silage sources (Fig. 2). It should be noted that changes in soil carbon due to changes in cropping system was not accounted for in the calculation of CF in this study due to un- availability of long-term data. A recent study by Little et al. (2017) concluded that AS-based system could potentially store more car- bon than CS-based dairy production systems. Future studies comparing two different forage based dairy production systems should account for reliable estimates of soil carbon change.

39: <header>3.2. Carbon footprint of milk for Holstein and Jersey breeds </header>

The CF of milk production for Holstein and Jersey breeds including the contribution of each process is shown in Table 5. Overall, the CF of milk production for Holsteins was 4.4% greater than Jerseys (1.47 VS. 1.41 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM). Holsteins had 29% greater manure management related GHG emissions than Jerseys (0.52 VS. 0.40 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM) but enteric CH4 intensity was 7% lower for the Holstein than Jersey (0.63 VS. 0.68 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM). Difference in enteric CH4 emissions between breeds was most likely associated with differences in FPCM. Like FNDF level, we found similar trend for cow breed when the contribution of each process was expressed as a percentage of overall CF i.e., the exis- tence of trade-off between enteric CH4 and manure management related GHG emissions again highlighting the importance of farm- level evaluations of mitigation strategies (Fig. 2). Additionally, the three major sources of GHG for both breeds were enteric CH4 fol- lowed by manure management and feed production related GHG emissions which comprised of approximately 92% GHG emissions for cradle-to-farmgate milk CF (Fig. 2). Very few studies have looked at the effects of cow breed on milk CF, however, past studies also showed a greater CF for Holstein than Jersey expressed either per unit of milk (Riva et al., 2014) or cheese production (Capper and Cady, 2012). Unlike our findings, both studies showed a substan- tially greater difference (20%) in CF between Holstein and Jersey

breeds mainly due to accounting for variation in herd structures whereas in our study we assumed a similar herd structure for both breeds. Therefore, difference in CF between breeds found in our study may be associated with factors like milk production, feed efficiency, manure volume and manure characteristics. Neverthe- less, the difference in CF between breeds became substantial (~10%) when accounting for inherent differences in fertility and replace- ment rate between breeds (i.e., assuming different herd structure between breeds).

Very few studies have looked at the effects of cow breed on milk CF, however, past studies also showed a greater CF for Holstein than Jersey expressed either per unit of milk (Riva et al., 2014) or cheese production (Capper and Cady, 2012). Unlike our findings, both studies showed a substan- tially greater difference (20%) in CF between Holstein and Jersey

The CF of milk production for Holstein and Jersey breeds including the contribution of each process is shown in Table 5. 42: Results shown in Fig. 3 indicated that all input parameters tested here had low to medium sensitivity (i.e., sensitivity index < 0.60, Rotz et al., 2020). Enteric CH4, manure CH4 during storage, manure N20 from field and feed production related GHG were found as the most influential parameters in our study conditions (Fig. 3). Enteric CH4 and manure chain emissions used in this study were measured in companion studies (i.e., empirical data) and thus were deemed high quality data with a minimum but expectedly similar uncer- tainty across scenarios due to using same protocol and equipment
Fig. 3. Sensitivity analysis (averaged sensitivity index across scenarios) showing the impact of emission sources on milk carbon footprint [kg CO2-e/kg fat-and-protein corrected milk (FPCM)].

to measure them (Wolf et al., 2016). Feed production related emission factors that were calculated using site-specific informa- tion were considered medium quality data. Other inventory data such as feed intake of animals, feed composition, milk yield and composition, manure yield and composition which were also a source of uncertainty, were site-specific and primary data (i.e., high quality with minimal uncertainty). Methodological uncertainties were expected to be the same across scenarios due to using the same methodologies. The standard deviation and 95% confidence interval of milk CF for dietary and breed scenarios are reported in Tables 4 and 5, respectively. The range of uncertainties were the same across dietary or breed scenarios due to the fact that we used same standard deviations as input parameters for all scenarios. The average coefficient of variation for milk CF was 7.1% (ranging from 6.2 to 7.8%) across scenarios.

44: <header>3.4. Effects of data source and emission allocation methods </header>

Our average CF result across scenarios for milk production under a Wisconsin production system was greater than the national level milk CF reported by Thoma et al. (2013) for the United States (1.43 VS. 1.23 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM). However, comparison of results among LCA studies is challenging and caution should be adopted before making any conclusions. Because, LCA results vary significantly across studies due to variability during goal and scope definition including LCA methods (Zanten et al., 2018), allocation among co- products (Little et al., 2017), system boundary (Thoma et al., 2013), source of inventory and emission data (Baldini et al., 2018), LCA assumptions (Wattiaux et al., 2019) and prevailing production systems (Battini et al., 2016). Our analysis revealed a substantial impact of GHG allocation methods on milk CF. Depending on the allocation method, the CF of milk production varied from 1.43 to 1.60 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM (average of four dietary scenarios; Supplementary Fig. S1). As expected, the CF was greatest (1.60 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM) when 100% emissions were allocated to milk whereas the CF was lowest (1.43 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM) when emis- sions were allocated to milk and meat using biophysical allocation as per IDF (2015) default method. In agreement with our results, Battini et al. (2016) also reported the similar magnitude and trend of CF for Italian production systems when the same three allocation methods were compared. These comparisons indicated the considerable influence of production system on milk CF. Inventory data source and emissions factors might have played an important role to overinflate our CF value compared to other studies. Because in our study we used site-specific inventory data and measured emissions factors for enteric CH4 and manure management GHG emissions gathered from companion studies whereas most of the studies cited here used either IPCC- or literature-based equations to estimate enteric CH4 and manure management GHG. A recent study also concluded that IPCC Tier 2 model overpredicted enteric CH4 production by 22% for the United States dataset (Niu et al., 2020). On contrary, Baldini et al. (2018) clearly showed that IPCC equations underestimated manure management related GHG by 21% when compared with measured GHG emissions. Thus, site-specific in- ventory data and measured emissions for enteric CH4 and manure management GHG might have influenced our findings. Most importantly, assumptions made in our study in comparison to cited studies might have also led the discrepancies in LCA results among studies which has been discussed in section 3.5.
3.5. Assumptions, limitations, and strengths of our study
Our assumption for herd structure (e.g., primiparous cows only) might have overinflated our estimate of milk CF since primiparous cows have lower milk production than a typical herd consisting of

both primiparous and multiparous cows. Our LCA study was unique in a sense that we used site-specific inventory data and measured emission for enteric CH4 and manure management GHG. We assumed that all the lactating COWS in the herd were primiparous due to availability of measurements for primiparous cows only. This assumption was one of the limitations in this study because in real- world farms dairy herd includes both primiparous and multiparous cows (typically from 30 to 40% of the former and 60-70% of the latter). Therefore, we performed a sensitivity analysis using the Holstein breed scenario to determine the effect of inclusion of multiparous cows in the herd (i.e., herd with 40% primiparous and 60% multiparous cows) on CF through accounting dry matter intake and milk production differences between primiparous and multiparous cows. Our sensitivity results showed that regardless of allocation method, Holstein herd composed of only primiparous lactating cows had approximately 7.5% greater CF than the Holstein herd composed of both primiparous and multiparous cows (Supplementary Fig. S2). This GHG reduction of 0.11 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM was mainly due to 22-27% greater milk production of multiparous cows when compared to primiparous COWS (Siewert et al., 2019). A review study based on 30 published LCAs found a reduction of 0.062 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM when milk yield was increased by 10% (Lorenz et al., 2019). The magnitude of GHG reduction in aforementioned study would be very similar to our findings when adjusted for average milk production differences (~22%) between primiparous and multiparous cows. Therefore, this difference needs to be accounted for while comparing our CF re- sults with other studies that mimic actual herd structure consisting of both primiparous and multiparous cows.

47: Overall, CF was 1.43 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM when using biophysical allocation and the CF was increased to 1.50 kg CO2-e/kg FPCM when using economic allocation. We assumed the herd consisted only of primiparous cows, thus, the CF was further reduced by 7.5% when multiparous cows were incorporated in the herd to simulate the real-world scenario. Our study demonstrated a substantial impact of forage level as LF-fed COWS had approximately 10% greater CF than HF-fed cows. These results may have been associated in part with differential inclusion of highly digestible non-forage fiber in the form of soyhulls in the diets. In contrast, the impact of forage source was minimal as milk CF was 2.5% greater for CS-fed cows than AS-fed cows. Overall CF difference between FNDF level (LF VS. HF) or FNDF source (AS VS. CS) should be interpreted cautiously due to probable interactions between FNDF level and FNDF source. In the case of cow breed, milk from Holsteins had 4.4% greater CF than the milk from Jerseys when assumed similar herd structure be- tween breeds. Nevertheless, accounting for differences in fertility and replacement rate between breeds (i.e., assuming different herd structure) led to a 10% difference in CF between breeds. Under our study conditions, differences in CF due to enteric fermentation were minimal but differences in CF across diets became substantial when combining the effects of cow (enteric CH4) and manure (CH4 and N20) emissions. These differences were even exacerbated further when accounting for the emissions associated with on-farm feed production and purchased feed (CO2 and N20). This study highlights the need for an integrated approach to assess the effects of dietary manipulations on milk CF.
